title: How to Use a Wooden Spurtle for Sourdough Bagels and Breads
meta_description: Discover how to master sourdough bagels and breads using a wooden spurtle. Learn mixing, scraping, and baking tips with our 2‑piece spurtle set.
html:
When I first tried to perfect my sourdough bagels and breads, the biggest hurdle wasn’t the starter—it was finding the right tool to stir, blend, and scrape without bruising the dough. A wooden spurtle, especially the 2 PCS Wooden Spurtle Spatula for Sourdough Starter, Wood Danish Dough Whisk, changed the game. Its thin, tapered shape gives you control over every crumb, making it ideal for both starter maintenance and dough handling.

Why a Wooden Spurtle Beats a Traditional Spoon

Most bakers reach for a metal spoon or a plastic whisk, but those tools can leave scratches on glass jars and don’t reach into the tight corners of a mixing bowl. The wooden spurtle’s smooth surface is non‑scratch, and the natural wood absorbs some of the dough’s moisture, preventing the stickiness that often clings to metal. This is especially helpful when you’re working with high‑hydration sourdough starter for bagels, where a clean scrape can save you a lot of waste.

Preparing Your Sourdough Starter with the Spurtle

Before you even think about shaping bagels or loaves, you need a vigorous starter. Here’s how the spurtle helps:

  1. Feed the starter: Add equal parts flour and water to your glass jar.
  2. Stir confidently: Use the skinny wooden spurtle to incorporate the flour fully, moving in a circular motion that reaches the bottom and sides.
  3. Scrape the jar: After mixing, run the spurtle along the glass to lift any stubborn bits – the wooden tip won’t chip the container.
  4. Cover and rest: Let the starter sit at room temperature until it bubbles, typically 4–6 hours.

Because the spurtle is lightweight, you can stir for the full minute without fatigue, ensuring an even distribution of wild yeast and bacteria.

Mixing Dough for Bagels and Breads

When it’s time to bring the starter into the final dough, the wooden spurtle shines again. Its narrow profile allows you to fold and turn the dough in the bowl without over‑developing gluten—a common pitfall when using a large spoon. For sourdough bagels, where a firm dough is essential, the spurtle gives you the tactile feedback needed to gauge consistency.

Steps for a basic sourdough bagel dough:

  • Combine starter, water, and malt syrup in a mixing bowl.
  • Use the spurtle to stir until the mixture is homogenous.
  • Add high‑protein flour and salt; continue stirring until a shaggy mass forms.
  • Switch to a brief hand‑knead; the spurtle can be used to break up any dry spots before the first turn.

The same technique applies to a classic sourdough loaf, only the hydration level is higher. The spurtle’s ability to glide through sticky dough without tearing makes the difference between a smooth, cohesive loaf and a dough that tears on the bench.

Shaping, Scraping, and Baking

After a bulk fermentation, you’ll shape bagels or a loaf. The spurtle’s tapered end is perfect for a final scrape of the work surface, ensuring every last bit of dough makes it into the pot. When you’re ready to bake, you can also use it to lift the dough onto a parchment‑lined baking sheet without disrupting its surface tension.

One tip for bagels: after boiling, give the dough a quick brush with the spurtle to remove excess water before placing them in a hot oven. The wooden handle won’t conduct heat, so you can safely maneuver the bagels on the hot stone.

Caring for Your Wooden Spurtle

To keep the spurtle performing like new, rinse it with warm water after each use and dry it promptly. A light rub with food‑grade mineral oil once a month prevents the wood from drying out and maintains its non‑stick qualities. Avoid soaking it or putting it in the dishwasher; the wood will warp and lose its smooth finish.

FAQ

Can I use the wooden spurtle with metal bowls?

Yes. The spurtle’s thin profile works well in both glass and metal bowls, and because it’s wood, it won’t scratch or react with acidic doughs.

Is the spurtle suitable for high‑hydration doughs?

Absolutely. Its tapered tip can navigate sticky, wet doughs that often cling to plastic tools, allowing you to mix and scrape efficiently.

How long will the wooden spurtle last?

With proper care—rinsing, drying, and occasional oiling—it can last for years, even with daily use in sourdough baking.
